questionText: A curve is defined by $x = 4\sec t$ and $y = 3\tan t$, for $0 \le t < 2\pi$ with $t \neq \dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$. Use the identity $1 + \tan^{2} t = \sec^{2} t$ to find a Cartesian equation of the curve, and describe the set of possible $x$-values.

steps.description: Make sec t and tan t the subjects
steps.explanation: To use the identity we need $\sec t$ and $\tan t$ by themselves. Dividing each equation by its coefficient isolates them, ready to substitute.
This identity holds for every valid $t$, so it is the bridge that lets us remove $t$ and keep only $x$ and $y$.
Moving $\tan^{2} t$ to the other side gives a difference of squares equal to $1$, which matches the form we get after substituting.

steps.description: Substitute the x and y expressions
steps.explanation: Replacing $\sec t$ and $\tan t$ with $\dfrac{x}{4}$ and $\dfrac{y}{3}$ turns the identity into a relation between $x$ and $y$ alone.
Squaring the fractions gives the standard form. The denominators $16$ and $9$ are the squares of the coefficients $4$ and $3$.
A difference of two squared terms equal to $1$ is the equation of a hyperbola centred at the origin, opening left and right.
Since $|\cos t| \leq 1$, its reciprocal $|\sec t|$ is always at least $1$. This controls how far $x$ can range.
Multiplying by $4$ shows the magnitude of $x$ is never less than $4$, so $x$ stays outside the strip between $-4$ and $4$.
The condition $|x| \geq 4$ splits into two intervals, one for each side of the origin.
The hyperbola has two separate branches; $\sec t > 0$ gives the right branch and $\sec t < 0$ gives the left branch.
This is the Cartesian equation with the allowed $x$-values, completing the description of the curve.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $\dfrac{x^{2}}{16} - \dfrac{y^{2}}{9} = 1$, a hyperbola centred at the origin; the possible $x$-values are $x \leq -4$ or $x \geq 4$.
